text: Demonstrated here is a single-port robotic approach to trans-anion minimally invasive surgery, or SPR-TAMIS. I have no disclosures.

The single-port robotic system is deployed and positioned at the operative field. The robotic boom rotates 360 degrees around the cannula's remote center, allowing access to all quadrants. The robot is docked to a 25mm multi-channel trocar containing a 3D camera and three working arms. The camera is inserted first, followed by the working arms, which are recognized by the robot and advanced into the operative field. The SP trocar is inserted through a transanal access platform.

The surgeon uses the full wrist and elbow articulation of the three robotic working arms to identify and mark a lesion with monopolar scissors. A holographic image at the six o'clock position displays the camera and instrument positions, aiding spatial awareness. The operative field is viewed in 3D as the surgeon enters the plane between the muscularis propria and perirectal fat.

The surgeon maintains the mesorectum intact while using fine articulation of the robotic instruments to orient the scissors for precise cutting. A blood vessel is visible entering through the muscularis propria into the perirectal fat. Sharp dissection is performed in a full thickness manner around the lesion's circumference. The holographic image indicates the use of a third arm for retraction, aiding in lesion orientation and access for resection.

The video shows the different layers of the rectal wall, including the mucosa, submucosa, muscularis propria, and perirectal fat. The inner circular and outer longitudinal layers of the muscularis propria are clearly visible. A bipolar instrument is used for retraction, demonstrating its potential for coagulation if needed.

The lesion located in the distal rectum is addressed using the robot, which provides access to the upper rectum if necessary. Residual blood supply to the disc excision is transected with monopolar scissors. The surgical defect is visible and confirmed to be hemostatic.

After irrigation with dilute betadine solution, the assistant port is used to pass a 3-0 vicryl stitch into the operative field. The SP robotic arms facilitate a full thickness closure, with the third arm retracting for optimal visualization. The defect is closed using a running full thickness suture, with full thickness bites shown clearly. Two hands tighten the suture while the third arm retracts. The scissors are loaded to cut the suture, completing the operation.
The specimen is displayed with clean margins, indicating successful excision. The surgical field appears clear and well-managed.
